rationale: The feature matrix directly includes a future return created with a negative shift, so the model can see the prediction horizon.
The next-day return is shifted backward and used as an input feature, leaking future price movement into training.
The trading signal is generated from a future volume change that would not be known at decision time, and the reported strategy return is gross of trading costs.
The shifted future return is both the basis for the target and included in the feature list, creating target leakage.
The z-score uses full-sample mean and standard deviation before train/test separation, so test-period distribution information leaks into training.
StandardScaler is fit on the full dataset before chronological splitting, which contaminates the training transformation.
The min and max values are computed on all rows before the test set is separated, leaking future extrema.
The market-cap z-score is computed using full-sample statistics before the date cutoff is applied.
A shuffled train_test_split breaks the chronological structure needed for a financial time-series evaluation.
Sorting by date is undone by a shuffled random split, so training can include observations after test observations.
The return classifier uses a shuffled train/test split on time-indexed market observations.
The backtest evaluation randomly divides dated rows, which invalidates chronological out-of-sample testing.
The backtest reports strategy returns and Sharpe ratio without fees, slippage, commissions, or turnover costs.
The strategy annualizes gross returns without modeling trading frictions.
A high-turnover signal is evaluated with gross strategy returns and no cost adjustment.
A transaction cost variable is declared but never subtracted from strategy returns, making this an intentional subtle failure case.
The writeup claims state-of-the-art performance and omits baselines, so the conclusion is not supported by evidence.
The writeup asserts outperformance from a chart without providing a baseline table or statistical support.
The writeup generalizes from a short BTC sample to live trading superiority without baselines or robustness checks.
The signal uses lagged information, applies an explicit turnover cost, and makes no unsupported performance claim.
The scaler is fit only on the training window and then applied to train and test separately.
The split preserves chronological order and does not use future features or global normalization.
The report frames the sample descriptively, includes a baseline reference, and avoids exaggerated unsupported claims.
The strategy uses lagged inputs and subtracts turnover-based trading costs before computing Sharpe.
The notebook computes market returns and multiplies them by a one-day lagged signal, which avoids same-day signal execution.
Both the trading signal and volatility target are shifted by one day before returns are computed, so the position uses information available before the return period.
The notebook generates a full performance report and Sharpe ratio on pre-cost strategy returns without subtracting brokerage, spread, slippage, or turnover costs.
The notebook explicitly combines slippage, spread, and brokerage into total costs, subtracts them from strategy returns, and compares Sharpe before and after costs.

This is an ambiguous notebook-derived case: the negative shift aligns turnover with the execution date for cost accounting rather than creating a predictive feature, so it should not be labeled as lookahead leakage.
The stock workflow uses adjusted close prices, lagged momentum, and an explicit turnover cost before computing strategy returns.
The feature set includes a next-day adjusted return built with a negative shift, leaking future stock returns into model training.
The adjusted-close z-score is computed with full-sample mean and standard deviation before the date split.
A shuffled train_test_split mixes dates in a stock time-series panel and can train on observations that occur after test rows.
The stock rotation strategy reports strategy_return and Sharpe without subtracting transaction costs, slippage, commissions, or turnover costs.
The writeup claims state-of-the-art stock performance and explicitly provides no baseline or evidence beyond visual inspection.
The cross-sectional rank feature is built from next-day adjusted returns, so the model sees future return information.
The strategy ranks stocks using future returns before assigning signals, and it also reports gross strategy returns without transaction costs.
StandardScaler is fit_transform'ed on the full stock panel before the train/test split, leaking test-period distribution information.
Volume normalization uses full-sample minimum and maximum before separating the date-based test set.
The multi-stock time-series feature table is randomly shuffled into train and test sets, breaking chronological evaluation.
The alpha validation randomly splits dated stock observations, allowing later dates into training while earlier dates appear in test.
The open-close strategy computes strategy returns and Sharpe without subtracting spreads, commissions, slippage, or turnover costs.
The daily rebalance strategy likely has nontrivial turnover but reports gross strategy returns and Sharpe without costs.
The report claims broad benchmark outperformance from a small selected stock sample and omits baseline comparisons.
The scaler is fit only on the chronological training window and then applied to test data, avoiding full-sample normalization leakage.
